name: CloudWatch Metrics and Alarms
description: Monitoring AWS resources with CloudWatch metrics, custom metrics, and composite alarms
key_facts: Default metrics collected at 5-minute intervals; detailed monitoring at 1-minute intervals
Custom metrics support high-resolution (1-second) publishing
Composite alarms combine multiple alarms with AND/OR logic
Metric math enables calculations across multiple metrics
Alarm states: OK, ALARM, INSUFFICIENT_DATA
common_misconceptions: EC2 memory and disk metrics are collected by default (they require the CloudWatch agent)
Detailed monitoring is free (it incurs additional charges)

name: CloudWatch Dashboards and Logs
description: Creating operational dashboards and analyzing log data with CloudWatch Logs Insights
key_facts: Dashboards support cross-account and cross-region widgets
CloudWatch Logs Insights provides SQL-like query language
Log groups have configurable retention periods (1 day to 10 years or never expire)
Metric filters extract metric data from log events
Subscription filters stream logs to Lambda, Kinesis, or Firehose
common_misconceptions: CloudWatch Logs are retained indefinitely by default (default is never expire, but best practice is to set retention)
Dashboards are limited to a single account (they support cross-account)

name: CloudTrail
description: API activity logging for governance, compliance, and security auditing
key_facts: Records API calls as events in JSON format
Management events logged by default; data events require explicit configuration
Organization trail captures events across all accounts
Log file integrity validation using SHA-256 hashing
Events delivered to S3 with optional SNS notification
common_misconceptions: CloudTrail logs data events (S3 object-level, Lambda invocations) by default (it does not)
CloudTrail is the same as CloudWatch Logs (CloudTrail is for API auditing, CloudWatch Logs is for application/system logs)

name: EventBridge
description: Serverless event bus for building event-driven architectures and automated responses
key_facts: Rules match incoming events and route to targets
Supports over 200 AWS service event sources
Scheduled rules replace CloudWatch Events cron/rate expressions
Event patterns support content-based filtering
Cross-account and cross-region event routing supported
common_misconceptions: EventBridge and CloudWatch Events are different services (EventBridge is the evolution of CloudWatch Events)
EventBridge only works with AWS services (it supports SaaS partners and custom applications)

name: AWS Backup and Recovery Strategies
description: Centralized backup management and disaster recovery planning across AWS services
key_facts: AWS Backup provides centralized, policy-based backup across services
Backup plans define schedule, retention, and lifecycle rules
Cross-region and cross-account backup copying supported
Backup Vault Lock enforces WORM (write-once-read-many) for compliance
Supports EBS, RDS, DynamoDB, EFS, FSx, EC2, and S3
common_misconceptions: AWS Backup replaces service-native snapshots (it orchestrates them, using native snapshot mechanisms)
Backup Vault Lock can be removed after being set (compliance mode cannot be removed)

name: RDS Multi-AZ and Read Replicas
description: High availability and read scaling for RDS databases
key_facts: Multi-AZ deploys a synchronous standby in a different AZ for automatic failover
Failover typically completes in 60-120 seconds
Read replicas use asynchronous replication for read scaling
Read replicas can be promoted to standalone instances
Multi-AZ does not improve read performance; use read replicas for that
common_misconceptions: Multi-AZ provides read scaling (it is for HA only, the standby is not readable)
Read replicas provide automatic failover (they require manual promotion)

name: Route 53 Health Checks and Failover
description: DNS-level health monitoring and automated failover routing
key_facts: Health checks monitor endpoint health, other health checks, or CloudWatch alarms
Failover routing policy routes traffic to a standby when primary is unhealthy
Calculated health checks combine results of multiple child health checks
Health check intervals: standard (30 seconds) or fast (10 seconds)
String matching health checks verify response body content
common_misconceptions: Route 53 health checks can monitor private endpoints directly (they check from public internet; use CloudWatch alarm-based checks for private resources)
Failover happens instantly (DNS TTL affects propagation time)

name: CloudFormation Stacks and Operations
description: Infrastructure as code provisioning with stack management and troubleshooting
key_facts: Stack creation rollback on failure is enabled by default
Change sets preview modifications before execution
Stack policies protect specific resources from unintended updates
Drift detection identifies out-of-band resource changes
DeletionPolicy: Retain, Snapshot, or Delete controls resource behavior on stack deletion
common_misconceptions: CloudFormation automatically detects drift (drift detection must be manually initiated)
Deleting a stack always deletes all resources (DeletionPolicy: Retain preserves resources)

name: AWS Systems Manager
description: Operational management for EC2 instances and on-premises servers
key_facts: SSM Agent enables remote management without SSH/RDP
Run Command executes commands across multiple instances
Patch Manager automates OS and application patching
Parameter Store provides hierarchical configuration and secrets storage
Session Manager provides auditable shell access without opening inbound ports
common_misconceptions: Systems Manager requires inbound SSH ports (Session Manager uses outbound HTTPS only)
Parameter Store and Secrets Manager are the same (Parameter Store is free for standard parameters; Secrets Manager supports automatic rotation)

name: Elastic Beanstalk Deployments
description: Managed application deployment with multiple deployment strategies
key_facts: All at once: fastest, causes downtime
Rolling: deploys in batches, reduced capacity during deployment
Rolling with additional batch: maintains full capacity
Immutable: deploys to new instances, safe rollback
Blue/green: uses separate environment with DNS swap via Route 53
common_misconceptions: Blue/green is a built-in Beanstalk deployment policy (it requires manual environment cloning and DNS swap)
Immutable deployments reuse existing instances (they launch entirely new instances)

name: IAM Policies and Access Management
description: Managing IAM policies, roles, and access for operational security
key_facts: Policy evaluation: explicit deny overrides any allow
IAM Access Analyzer identifies resources shared with external entities
Credential Report lists all IAM users and credential status
IAM policy simulator tests policy effects before deployment
Service-linked roles are predefined by AWS services
common_misconceptions: An explicit allow overrides an explicit deny (deny always wins)
IAM Access Analyzer only checks IAM policies (it also checks S3, KMS, SQS, Lambda, and Secrets Manager resource policies)

name: AWS Config Rules and Remediation
description: Continuous compliance monitoring with managed and custom Config rules
key_facts: Config records resource configurations and changes over time
Managed rules cover common compliance checks (e.g., encrypted EBS, public S3)
Custom rules use Lambda functions for organization-specific checks
Automatic remediation via SSM Automation documents
Conformance packs bundle multiple rules for compliance frameworks
common_misconceptions: AWS Config prevents non-compliant changes (it detects and can remediate but does not prevent)
Config rules evaluate only on schedule (they can be triggered by configuration changes or on a schedule)

name: Patch Management and Inspector
description: Automated patching workflows and vulnerability assessment
key_facts: SSM Patch Manager uses patch baselines to define approved patches
Maintenance windows schedule patching during off-peak hours
Patch compliance reports show instance patch status
Amazon Inspector scans EC2 instances and container images for vulnerabilities
Inspector integrates with EventBridge for automated remediation workflows
common_misconceptions: Patch Manager automatically patches all instances (it requires patch baselines and maintenance windows to be configured)
Inspector is the same as GuardDuty (Inspector finds vulnerabilities; GuardDuty detects threats)

name: Elastic Load Balancer Types
description: Selecting and configuring ALB, NLB, and GWLB for different use cases
key_facts: ALB: Layer 7, HTTP/HTTPS, path/host-based routing, WebSocket support
NLB: Layer 4, TCP/UDP/TLS, ultra-low latency, static IP per AZ
GWLB: Layer 3, transparent network gateway for third-party appliances
ALB supports weighted target groups for blue/green deployments
Cross-zone load balancing is enabled by default for ALB, optional for NLB
common_misconceptions: NLB supports path-based routing (only ALB does, NLB operates at Layer 4)
ALB can provide a static IP address (ALB uses dynamic IPs; use NLB or Global Accelerator for static IPs)

name: Route 53 Routing Policies
description: DNS routing strategies for traffic management and high availability
key_facts: Simple: single resource, no health checks
Weighted: distribute traffic by percentage across resources
Latency-based: route to the lowest-latency region
Failover: active-passive with health check integration
Geolocation: route based on user geographic location
Multivalue answer: return multiple healthy records (up to 8)
common_misconceptions: Geolocation and latency-based routing are the same (geolocation routes by location, latency routes by measured latency)
Multivalue answer is a replacement for a load balancer (it provides client-side DNS-level distribution, not true load balancing)

name: Auto Scaling Policies
description: EC2 Auto Scaling group configuration and scaling policy types
key_facts: Target tracking: maintains a specific metric value (e.g., 50% CPU)
Step scaling: adjusts capacity in steps based on alarm thresholds
Scheduled scaling: adjusts capacity at specific times
Predictive scaling: uses ML to forecast and pre-scale
Cooldown periods prevent rapid scale-in/scale-out oscillation
common_misconceptions: Auto Scaling only scales out (it also scales in to reduce costs)
Target tracking and step scaling should be used together on the same metric (this causes conflicts)

name: Trusted Advisor and Cost Optimization
description: Using Trusted Advisor and Compute Optimizer for cost and performance recommendations
key_facts: Trusted Advisor checks: cost optimization, performance, security, fault tolerance, service limits
Basic/Developer support: 7 core checks (S3 permissions, MFA on root, etc.)
Business/Enterprise support: full set of checks with API access
Compute Optimizer recommends optimal EC2, EBS, Lambda, and ECS configurations
Compute Optimizer requires at least 30 hours of metrics to generate recommendations
common_misconceptions: All Trusted Advisor checks are available on all support plans (most require Business or Enterprise support)
Compute Optimizer and Trusted Advisor provide the same recommendations (Compute Optimizer is ML-based and more granular for compute resources)
